section.title: The Healing of Naaman
section.sectionRationale: A powerful Aramean commander with leprosy is directed by a captured Israelite girl toward Elisha, and after resisting the prophet's humble prescription, washes in the Jordan and is healed, confessing the LORD as the only true God. The chapter closes with Elisha's servant Gehazi secretly seizing payment Elisha refused, and inheriting Naaman's leprosy as judgment.
section.thesis: The LORD freely heals and saves a pagan foreigner who humbles himself to obey a simple word, while His own servant, grasping for gain, receives the very disease grace had just removed, showing that the LORD's blessing is received by humble faith, not achieved by status or seized by greed.
section.frameNote: Four beats: Naaman's condition and the servant girl's word, verses 1 to 7; Elisha's prescription and Naaman's initial rage, verses 8 to 14; Naaman's confession and Elisha's refusal of payment, verses 15 to 19; and Gehazi's greed and resulting leprosy, verses 20 to 27.

units.label: Naaman's condition and the servant girl's word
units.note: there is a prophet in Samaria

verses.text: Now Naaman, the commander of the army of the king of Aram, was a great man in his master's sight and highly regarded, for through him the LORD had given victory to Aram. And he was a mighty man of valor, but he was a leper.
At this time the Arameans had gone out in bands and had taken a young girl from the land of Israel, and she was serving Naaman's wife.
She said to her mistress, "If only my master would go to the prophet who is in Samaria, he would cure him of his {{tsaraath|leprosy}}."
And Naaman went and told his master what the girl from the land of Israel had said.

"Go now," said the king of Aram, "and I will send you with a letter to the king of Israel." So Naaman departed, taking with him ten talents of silver, six thousand shekels of gold, and ten sets of clothing.
And the letter that he took to the king of Israel stated: "With this letter I am sending my servant Naaman, so that you may cure him of his leprosy."

When the king of Israel read the letter, he tore his clothes and asked, "Am I God, killing and giving life, that this man expects me to cure a leper? Surely you can see that he is seeking a quarrel with me!"

Now when Elisha the man of God heard that the king of Israel had torn his clothes, he sent a message to the king: "Why have you torn your clothes? Please let the man come to me, and he will know that there is a prophet in Israel."
So Naaman came with his horses and chariots and stood at the door of Elisha's house.
Then Elisha sent him a messenger, who said, "Go and wash yourself seven times in the Jordan, and your flesh will be restored, and you will be clean."
But Naaman went away angry, saying, "I thought that he would surely come out, stand and call on the name of the LORD his God, and wave his hand over the spot to cure my leprosy.
Are not the Abanah and Pharpar, the rivers of Damascus, better than all the waters of Israel? Could I not have washed in them and been cleansed?" So he turned and went away in a rage.

Naaman's servants, however, approached him and said, "My father, if the prophet had told you to do some great thing, would you not have done it? How much more, then, when he tells you, 'Wash and be cleansed'?"
So Naaman went down and dipped himself in the Jordan seven times, according to the word of the man of God, and his flesh was restored and became like that of a little child, and he was clean.

units.label: Naaman's confession and Elisha's refusal
units.note: there is no God in all the earth except in Israel

verses.text: Then Naaman and all his attendants went back to the man of God, stood before him, and declared, "Now I know for sure that there is no God in all the earth except in Israel. So please accept a gift from your servant."
But Elisha replied, "As surely as the LORD lives, before whom I stand, I will not accept it." And although Naaman urged him to accept it, he refused.

"If you will not," said Naaman, "please let me, your {{eved|servant}}, be given as much {{adamah|soil}} as a pair of mules can carry. For your servant will never again make a burnt offering or a sacrifice to any other god but the LORD.

Yet may the LORD forgive your servant this one thing: When my master goes into the temple of Rimmon to worship there, and he leans on my arm, and I bow down in the temple of Rimmon, may the LORD forgive your servant in this matter."
"Go in {{shalom|peace}}," said Elisha. But after Naaman had traveled a short distance,

units.label: Gehazi's greed and leprosy
units.note: the leprosy of Naaman will cling to you

verses.text: Gehazi, the servant of Elisha the man of God, said, "Look, my master has spared this Aramean, Naaman, by not accepting what he brought. As surely as the LORD lives, I will run after him and get something from him."
So Gehazi pursued Naaman. And when Naaman saw him running toward him, he got down from the chariot to meet him and asked, "Is everything all right?"

"Everything is all right," Gehazi replied. "My master has sent me to say, 'Look, two young men from the sons of the prophets have just now come to me from the hill country of Ephraim. Please give them a talent of silver and two sets of clothing.'"

But Naaman insisted, "Please, take two talents." And he urged Gehazi to accept them. Then he tied up two talents of silver in two bags along with two sets of clothing and gave them to two of his servants, who carried them ahead of Gehazi.
When Gehazi came to the hill, he took the gifts from the servants and stored them in the house. Then he dismissed the men, and they departed.
When Gehazi went in and stood before his master, Elisha asked him, "Gehazi, where have you been?" "Your servant did not go anywhere," he replied.

But Elisha questioned him, "Did not my spirit go with you when the man got down from his chariot to meet you? Is this the time to accept money and clothing, olive groves and vineyards, sheep and oxen, menservants and maidservants?
Therefore, the leprosy of Naaman will cling to you and your descendants forever!" And as Gehazi left his presence, he was leprous, as white as snow.

tsaraath.gloss: A term for various skin diseases translated leprosy, treated at length in Leviticus 13 to 14 as ritually defiling. It afflicts a celebrated pagan general and, by the chapter's end, Elisha's own greedy servant, showing that no rank exempts anyone from needing the LORD's cleansing.

adamah.gloss: Soil, earth, or ground. Naaman's request for Israelite soil reflects an ancient belief that a god was tied to his land; taking earth home was likely meant to let him worship the LORD on His own ground even while living in Aram, an early, imperfect step of a genuine new faith.

shalom.gloss: Peace, wholeness, or well being. Elisha's parting words to Naaman, go in shalom, release the healed foreigner in blessing despite the moral complexity of his continuing court duties, entrusting him fully to the LORD's mercy rather than imposing a rigid demand.

eved.gloss: Servant or slave. Naaman repeatedly calls himself Elisha's eved after his healing, a striking reversal for a powerful commander, and the same word Gehazi twice denies being sent as, your servant did not go anywhere, exposing his lie even as he claims the title honestly belongs to him under Elisha.

basic.context: Naaman, a celebrated Aramean general, has leprosy, and a young Israelite captive girl points him toward Elisha. The king of Israel panics at the request to heal him, but Elisha calls Naaman to himself. Offended that Elisha does not personally perform a dramatic ritual, Naaman nearly leaves in anger, but his own servants persuade him to try the simple instruction: wash seven times in the Jordan. He is healed, confesses the LORD as the only true God, and offers payment, which Elisha refuses. Naaman asks to take Israelite soil home and pre-emptively asks pardon for a compromise he foresees. Elisha's servant Gehazi, however, secretly chases Naaman down, lies to obtain payment, lies again to Elisha, and receives Naaman's leprosy as judgment.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative describing a specific miraculous healing and its aftermath; it is not a proof that ritual washing itself heals disease, nor a general policy on compromise in worship. What endures is that God's grace reaches beyond Israel to a Gentile who humbles himself in simple obedience, and that grasping for what God has freely given, rather than trusting Him, brings serious consequences.
basic.originalLanguages: Naaman suffers tsaraath, a skin affliction rendered leprosy, the very disease that later falls on Elisha's own servant. His request for Israelite adamah, soil, reflects the ancient assumption that a god was bound to a particular land, an imperfect but genuine step of new faith. Elisha's dismissal, go in shalom, releases Naaman in peace despite unresolved moral tension, while Gehazi's repeated claim to be no one's eved, servant, when questioned, unmasks the very lie that costs him everything.
basic.typology: Naaman's cleansing through simple, humble obedience to a word rather than an impressive ritual anticipates the pattern of salvation by faith rather than by human achievement or status, while Gehazi's grasping after what grace had freely refused warns against turning God's gifts into personal gain.
basic.christ: Naaman's healing, received only when he humbled himself to obey a simple word rather than demand a dramatic display fitting his rank, pictures how salvation comes not to the impressive but to the humble who simply trust and obey what Christ says, as Paul later reasons that the gospel confounds the proud and saves through what seems foolish to the world, 1 Corinthians 1:26-29. Naaman, a Gentile outsider, is cleansed while many lepers in Israel were not, a truth Jesus Himself cites in Luke 4:27 to explain why God's grace was never confined to ethnic Israel alone.
basic.god: God is free to extend His saving mercy to a foreign enemy general, showing His grace is not confined to Israel by birth but reaches all who humble themselves before Him. He is also holy and cannot be used for personal profit; Elisha's refusal of payment protects the truth that God's grace is free, and Gehazi's judgment shows that greed dressed in religious service still meets real consequences.
basic.crossReferences: Luke 4:27 has Jesus cite Naaman's healing to explain God's grace reaching beyond Israel's own borders even in the Old Testament. Leviticus 14 provides the background ritual law for skin disease that this account presupposes and, in a sense, bypasses through simple obedient faith. Acts 8:18-24 tells of Simon trying to buy the gift of the Spirit, a parallel warning against treating God's grace as a commodity.
basic.oneStory: A proud Gentile commander is healed and brought to confess the LORD alone as God through humble obedience, while a grasping insider forfeits blessing through greed, together previewing the gospel pattern: God's grace reaches the humble outsider by faith, while those who try to seize or commercialize what is freely given find only judgment.

context.literary: The chapter is carefully built around contrasts: a captive servant girl's simple faith versus a panicked king's despair, Naaman's initial pride versus his servants' wise counsel, and finally Naaman's humble confession versus Gehazi's secret greed, so that the healed outsider ends the story more faithful than the prophet's own household member.
context.historical: Aram, based in Damascus, was a regular rival and sometime overlord of Israel in this period; a captured Israelite girl serving in an enemy general's household reflects the ongoing border raids of the era. Skin disease, tsaraath, carried serious social and religious consequences under Israelite law, Leviticus 13, making Naaman's affliction and subsequent healing publicly significant.
context.canonical: This account stands as one of the clearest Old Testament pictures of God's grace extended to a Gentile, cited directly by Jesus in Luke 4:27, and it also continues the pattern from chapter 4 of Elisha's ministry meeting concrete need, while introducing Gehazi's moral failure that will shape his role later in chapter 8.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that the LORD's grace reaches beyond Israel to a humbled Gentile who obeys a simple word, while warning that greedily seizing what God's grace has freely given, rather than trusting Him, invites serious judgment.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes one specific healing and its consequences; it does not prescribe washing in a river seven times as a religious ritual, nor does it settle every question of how far a convert may participate in surrounding cultural or religious structures. What endures is the pattern of humble faith versus grasping pride.

debates.question: Did Elisha's answer, go in peace, in verse 19 approve Naaman's anticipated continued attendance at the temple of Rimmon?

views.label: A pastoral accommodation for a young, imperfect convert
views.case: Naaman has just come to genuine faith and remains bound by state duty to physically assist his king in a pagan temple; Elisha's blessing, without further rebuke, may reflect wise pastoral patience with a new believer still working out the practical implications of exclusive worship of the LORD in a pagan court.

views.label: A general blessing that leaves the specific moral question unresolved
views.case: Elisha's brief reply may simply be a customary farewell rather than a considered verdict on the ethics of Naaman's court duties, leaving the tension deliberately open rather than explicitly endorsing continued physical presence in idol worship.

debates.bearing: Either way the text does not present Naaman's situation as a normal pattern to imitate uncritically, but it does show God's grace meeting a genuine, if still developing, convert with patience rather than immediate demand for perfect separation.

study.originalLanguages: Naaman's tsaraath, skin disease, makes him ritually unclean and socially marked despite his military honor. His request for Israelite adamah, soil, reflects an ancient assumption that a god was tied to a particular land, an imperfect step toward exclusive worship of the LORD. Elisha's farewell, go in shalom, peace, releases him graciously, while Gehazi's repeated claim not to be anyone's eved, servant, when Elisha questions him, is precisely the lie that exposes and condemns him.

typology.type: A Gentile outsider cleansed through humble, simple obedience rather than status or ritual grandeur
typology.fulfillment: Salvation offered to all nations through simple faith in Christ's word rather than human merit or ceremony, Romans 10:9-13, Luke 4:25-27.

typology.type: Washing seven times in the Jordan to be made clean
typology.fulfillment: Baptism and the cleansing from sin accomplished through union with Christ, 1 Peter 3:21, Titus 3:5.

study.christ: Naaman is healed not by an impressive display befitting his rank but by humbling himself to obey Elisha's simple word, wash and be clean, prefiguring how the gospel saves not the proud who demand a sign but the humble who simply trust and obey Christ's word, as Jesus Himself points to Naaman to illustrate that God's grace was never limited to ethnic Israel, Luke 4:27. Where Naaman confesses there is no God in all the earth except in Israel, believers today confess that salvation is found in no one else but Jesus Christ, Acts 4:12, the true fulfillment of the God Naaman came to know.
study.god: God freely extends saving mercy to a foreign military commander, an enemy of Israel, showing His grace was never restricted to Israel by ethnicity alone. He is also a God who cannot be bought or used for personal profit; Elisha's refusal of Naaman's gift protects the truth that His grace is free, while Gehazi's punishment shows that greed masquerading as ministry brings real and lasting consequences.

crossReferences.note: Jesus cites Naaman's healing directly to show that God's grace in the Old Testament already reached beyond Israel's own borders.
Provides the background law on skin disease and ritual uncleanness that frames the seriousness of Naaman's, and later Gehazi's, affliction.
Simon the sorcerer's attempt to buy the gift of the Spirit parallels Gehazi's attempt to profit from what God had freely given through Elisha.
God's pattern of using what seems foolish or lowly to save people, echoed in the simple washing that heals a proud general.

study.oneStory: A proud Gentile general is humbled and healed through simple, obedient faith and confesses the LORD as the only true God, while Elisha's own servant, grasping in secret for the payment his master refused, receives the very disease grace had just removed. Together they preview the gospel pattern that runs through the whole Bible: God's free grace reaches humble outsiders by faith, while those who try to seize or sell what God freely gives find only judgment, until Christ, the true and greater healer, offers cleansing to all who will simply come and wash.

sermon.title: The Lepers Are Cleansed
sermon.why: Preached by Timothy J. Keller at Redeemer Presbyterian Church on October 10, 1999, in the series Receiving the Spirit (with Elisha), directly covering 2 Kings 5:1-19, the healing of Naaman.

quiz.q: Who first directed Naaman toward help for his leprosy, and what did she say?

options.text: A captured young Israelite girl serving Naaman's wife, who said the prophet in Samaria could cure him.
options.note: Verses 2 to 3 identify her exact role and words.

options.text: The king of Aram personally recommended Elisha.
options.note: The king only acted after hearing the report from Naaman, who had heard it from the servant girl.

options.text: Naaman's own physicians suggested he travel to Israel.
options.note: No physicians are mentioned; the suggestion came from the captive Israelite girl.

options.text: Elisha sent a personal invitation to Naaman.
options.note: Elisha only responded after hearing of the king of Israel's distress over Naaman's letter; he did not initiate contact.

quiz.q: Why did Naaman initially become enraged at Elisha's instructions?

options.text: He expected a dramatic ritual with the prophet calling on the LORD and waving his hand, and was offended by the simple command to wash in the Jordan.
options.note: Verses 11 to 12 record exactly this expectation and his resulting anger.

options.text: Elisha refused to see him at all.
options.note: Elisha did send him instructions through a messenger; the offense was the simplicity of the command, not a total refusal to engage.

options.text: He was told to pay an enormous sum of money first.
options.note: No payment was demanded before healing; Elisha later refused payment entirely.

options.text: He was told the healing was impossible.
options.note: Elisha's instructions promised healing through washing, not a declaration of impossibility.

quiz.q: What did Naaman confess after he was healed, and how did Elisha respond to his offered gift?

options.text: Naaman confessed there is no God in all the earth except in Israel, and Elisha refused to accept any payment despite being urged.
options.note: Verses 15 to 16 record both his confession and Elisha's firm refusal.

options.text: Naaman confessed his sins but Elisha eagerly accepted his gifts.
options.note: Elisha explicitly and repeatedly refused the gift, even when urged.

options.text: Naaman refused to acknowledge the LORD at all after being healed.
options.note: The text records an explicit, strong confession of the LORD as the only true God.

options.text: Elisha demanded double the payment Naaman had originally offered.
options.note: Elisha refused any payment whatsoever, the opposite of demanding more.

quiz.q: What two requests did Naaman make of Elisha before departing, and how did Elisha respond?

options.text: He asked for Israelite soil to worship the LORD on and pre-emptively asked forgiveness for future required attendance at the temple of Rimmon, and Elisha simply said, go in peace.
options.note: Verses 17 to 19 record both requests and Elisha's brief, gracious dismissal.

options.text: He asked to become an Israelite citizen and to marry an Israelite woman.
options.note: His actual requests concerned soil for worship and a specific moral tension regarding his court duties, not citizenship or marriage.

options.text: He asked Elisha to return with him to Aram permanently.
options.note: No such request is recorded; Naaman intended to return home to serve his own king.

options.text: He asked for nothing further and left immediately without conversation.
options.note: The text records a specific, detailed exchange between Naaman and Elisha before his departure.

quiz.q: What did Gehazi do after Naaman departed, and what was the consequence?

options.text: He secretly pursued Naaman, lied to obtain silver and clothing, then lied to Elisha about it, and as a result Naaman's leprosy came upon him and his descendants forever.
options.note: Verses 20 to 27 record his deception, his lie to Elisha, and the resulting judgment.

options.text: He openly asked Elisha's permission to accept a gift from Naaman.
options.note: Gehazi acted entirely in secret, without Elisha's knowledge or permission, which was central to his sin.

options.text: He refused any payment from Naaman out of respect for Elisha's example.
options.note: Gehazi did precisely the opposite, secretly pursuing payment his master had refused.

options.text: He was rewarded by Elisha for his resourcefulness.
options.note: Elisha pronounced Naaman's own leprosy upon Gehazi as judgment, not a reward.

questions: The captured servant girl, with little power of her own, still spoke a word of hope that changed Naaman's life. Where might God be asking you to speak up in faith even from a position that feels small or powerless?

Naaman nearly walked away from healing because it did not match what he expected from God. Where are you resisting a simple, humbling instruction from God because you wanted something more impressive?
Naaman confessed the LORD alone as God only after humbling himself to obey. What is one area where obedience, not just belief, is the next step God is asking of you?
Elisha refused payment so that grace would remain free and unmistakable. Where might you be tempted to attach strings, price, or personal gain to something God has called you to give freely?

Gehazi's secret greed cost him everything Naaman's public confession had gained. What hidden compromise in your own life needs to be brought into the light before it grows into something far more costly?

translationNotes.note: The BSB footnote clarifies that leper or leprosy in this chapter refers broadly to various skin diseases as categorized in Leviticus 13, not necessarily the specific modern medical condition called leprosy today.
The BSB footnotes convert the gifts Naaman brought to modern equivalents, approximately 754 pounds of silver and 150.8 pounds of gold, underscoring the enormous value he expected to pay for his cure.

Rimmon was a title associated with the Aramean storm god Hadad; Naaman's request for pardon reflects his ongoing court obligation to physically support his king even while attending pagan worship, a tension the text leaves without a direct resolution.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 2 Kings 5:1-27. No em dashes or en dashes appear in the source text for this chapter.
